title: varslice
text:
varslice loads and slices gridded variables (up to four dimensions: x, y, z, time) out of one or more netCDF files. It is the gridded cousin of the lightweight series module: where series handles 0-D time series, varslice handles full spatial fields that evolve over time.
The problem it solves is reading a slice of a large field without holding the whole thing in memory or writing bespoke nc_read calls at every call site:

Time slicing — given a target time (or time range), it reads only the required timesteps from disk and reduces them to the requested output via a configurable slice_method (exact match, range, interpolation, extrapolation, or a range statistic such as mean/min/max/standard deviation).
Sub-annual (subgrid) time — a variable may carry a fractional sub-axis (e.g. 12 monthly values per year). varslice builds the sub-annual time axis and lets a range statistic repeat over it (e.g. produce a 12-month climatology by applying a mean to every 12th index).
Multi-file matching — a single filename may be a glob pattern spanning many files; varslice expands it, concatenates the time dimension across files, and reads a contiguous span transparently.
Unit handling — an input variable is rescaled on read via unit_scale and unit_offset, with missing values preserved.

The variable is configured once (from a namelist or from arguments), then updated repeatedly to whatever time the calling model currently needs.

The types varslice_param_class and varslice_class are also public.

Signatures
! Initialize from a namelist group
subroutine varslice_init_nml(vs, filename, group, domain, grid_name, verbose)
    type(varslice_class), intent(INOUT) :: vs
    character(len=*),     intent(IN)    :: filename        ! namelist file
    character(len=*),     intent(IN)    :: group           ! namelist group name
    character(len=*),     intent(IN), optional :: domain    ! substitutes {domain}
    character(len=*),     intent(IN), optional :: grid_name ! substitutes {grid_name}
    logical,              intent(IN), optional :: verbose

! Initialize from explicit arguments (no namelist)
subroutine varslice_init_arg(vs, filename, name, units_in, units_out, &
                             scale, offset, with_time, time_par)
    type(varslice_class), intent(INOUT) :: vs
    character(len=*),     intent(IN)    :: filename, name, units_in, units_out
    real(wp), optional,   intent(IN)    :: scale, offset
    logical,  optional,   intent(IN)    :: with_time
    real(wp), optional,   intent(IN)    :: time_par(:)

! Update the field to a given time or time range
subroutine varslice_update(vs, time, method, fill, rep, print_summary)
    type(varslice_class),       intent(INOUT) :: vs
    real(wp),         optional, intent(IN)    :: time(:)       ! time or [t0,t1]
    character(len=*), optional, intent(IN)    :: method        ! slice_method
    character(len=*), optional, intent(IN)    :: fill          ! only "none" supported
    integer,          optional, intent(IN)    :: rep           ! repetition frequency
    logical,          optional, intent(IN)    :: print_summary
method (slice_method) selects how the read timesteps are reduced:

"exact" — the timestep matching time exactly (missing if unavailable).
"range" — all timesteps within [t0,t1], kept along the time dimension.
"interp" — linear interpolation to time from the two bracketing timesteps; missing if time is out of bounds.
"extrap" — like "interp", but clamps to the first/last timestep when out of bounds.
"range_mean", "range_min", "range_max", "range_sd", "range_sum" — a single slice computed as the mean / min / max / standard deviation / sum over [t0,t1].

rep (stored as range_rep) is the frequency at which a range method is applied. rep=1 reduces the whole range to one value; rep=12 applies the statistic to every 12th index, yielding 12 values along the time dimension (e.g. a monthly climatology).
fill currently only accepts "none"; other values abort (not yet implemented).

title: varslice
text:
Namelist configuration
varslice_init_nml reads the following keys from the given group (via nml_read in varslice_par_load):
Each key bundles related quantities so the group stays short while keeping the full provenance explicit in the file:








Key
Type
Meaning




filename
string
Source file or glob pattern; supports {domain} and {grid_name} placeholders


name
string
netCDF variable name


units
string(2)
"<units_in>" "<units_out>" (metadata only)


scaling
real(2)
<unit_scale> <unit_offset> applied on read: value*scale + offset


time
real(5)
<active> <x0> <x1> <dx> <n_sub> (see below)



Notes on time = [active, x0, x1, dx, n_sub]:

active flags time-dependence: 1.0 = the variable is time-varying; 0.0 = the variable is static and x0..x1 merely document the period the data represents (kept for provenance, never collapsed).
x0, x1, dx define the file’s time axis (used when active = 1.0); the generated axis must match the netCDF time length or init aborts.
When active = 1.0 and dx == 0, x1 is forced to x0 (single timestep).
n_sub is optional (trailing) and defaults to 0. If n_sub > 1, a sub-annual axis is built with n_sub steps per major unit (e.g. n_sub = 12 for monthly data) and with_time_sub is set .TRUE..

These keys unpack internally to par%units_in/units_out, par%unit_scale/unit_offset, par%with_time, and par%time_par(4).
Example namelist group:
&ts_series
    filename = "input/{domain}/{grid_name}_climate.nc"
    name     = "t2m"
    units    = "K" "degC"
    scaling  = 1.0 -273.15                     ! value*scale + offset
    time     = 1.0, -22000.0, 0.0, 1.0, 12.0   ! active, x0, x1, dx, n_sub (monthly)
/

title: varslice
text:
Usage example
use varslice, only : varslice_class, varslice_init_nml, varslice_update

type(varslice_class) :: vs
real(wp) :: time_now

! 1. Configure the variable from a namelist group (loads coords + time axis).
call varslice_init_nml(vs, "input_par.nml", group="ts_series", &
                       domain="Greenland", grid_name="GRL-16KM")

! 2. Update to a given time. Here: a 12-month climatology (mean over a
!    2000-year window, applied to every 12th sub-annual index).
time_now = -8000.0_wp
call varslice_update(vs, time=[time_now-1000.0_wp, time_now+1000.0_wp], &
                     method="range_mean", rep=12, print_summary=.TRUE.)

! 3. Use the result. vs%var is rank-4 (x, y, z, time); here z=1, time=1..12.
!    e.g. the January field:
!        vs%var(:,:,1,1)
!    or a single interpolated snapshot instead:
call varslice_update(vs, time=[time_now], method="interp")
!        vs%var(:,:,1,1)   ! interpolated 2-D field at time_now

title: timing
text: The fesm-utils library provides three small, independent modules that together handle the “time” bookkeeping of a model run: timer profiles how long code sections take, timeout decides which timesteps get written to output, and timestepping manages the model’s time axis and advances it each iteration. They are unrelated in implementation and can be used separately, but a typical main loop uses all three at once.

title: timing
text:
timer
timer measures wall-clock (or CPU) time spent in labelled sections of code so that a run can be profiled — how much time went to isostasy vs. ice dynamics vs. I/O — and reports both an end-of-run summary and an appendable timing-log table. Internally it prefers omp_get_wtime() when compiled with OpenMP and falls back to cpu_time() otherwise.

Public API
type timer_class
    ! Accumulates timing for up to ncomp_max = 100 components.
    ! All fields are managed internally; only the derived type is public.
end type
! Generic interface (dispatches on the optional time_mod argument type):
call timer_step(timer, comp [, time_mod] [, label])

  type(timer_class), intent(INOUT) :: timer
  integer,           intent(IN)    :: comp        ! component index; see below
  real(4|8),         intent(IN)    :: time_mod(2) ! optional [t_start, t_end] in model units
  character(len=*),  intent(IN)    :: label       ! optional name for this component
comp controls the action:

comp < 0 — reset all accumulated totals and start the clock.
comp == 0 — restart the clock without recording (marks the start of the next interval).
comp > 0 — record the elapsed time since the last call into component comp, optionally tagging it with label and the model-time span time_mod.

! Print a formatted per-component summary to standard output:
call timer_print_summary(timer, units, units_mod, time_mod)

  type(timer_class), intent(INOUT) :: timer
  character(len=1),  intent(IN)    :: units      ! "s", "m", or "h"
  character(len=*),  intent(IN)    :: units_mod  ! model time units label, e.g. "kyr"
  real(4|8),         intent(IN)    :: time_mod   ! current model time (scalar)
! Initialize (or overwrite) a timing-log table file with a header row:
call timer_write_table_init(timer, filename)

! Append one timing row (time, dt, per-component times, total, rate) to the file:
call timer_write_table(timer, time, units, filename [, init])

  type(timer_class), intent(IN) :: timer
  real(4|8),         intent(IN) :: time(2)   ! [time_now, dt_now] in model units
  character(len=1),  intent(IN) :: units     ! "s", "m", or "h"
  character(len=*),  intent(IN) :: filename
  logical,           intent(IN) :: init      ! optional; if .TRUE. (re)initialize the file first
The rate column is CPU-time per unit model-time (units/units_mod). Timing units "s", "m", "h" are seconds, minutes, hours; any other value yields -1.


Usage
Wrap each section of the loop between timer_step calls, then summarize at the end:
type(timer_class) :: tmr, tmrs

call timer_step(tmr, comp=-1)                        ! reset outer timer, start clock
! ... initialization ...
call timer_step(tmr, comp=1, label="initialization")

call timer_step(tmrs, comp=-1)                       ! reset inner (per-step) timer
do while (.not. ts%is_finished)
    call timer_step(tmrs, comp=0)                    ! restart clock for this step
    ! ... isostasy ...
    call timer_step(tmrs, comp=1, time_mod=[t0,t1], label="isostasy")
    ! ... ice dynamics ...
    call timer_step(tmrs, comp=2, time_mod=[t0,t1], label="yelmo")
    ! ... I/O ...
    call timer_step(tmrs, comp=3, time_mod=[t0,t1], label="io")

    call timer_write_table(tmrs, [ts%time, dtt]*1e-3, "m", tmr_file, &
                           init=ts%time_elapsed .eq. 0.0)
end do

call timer_step(tmr, comp=2, time_mod=[t_init,ts%time]*1e-3, label="timeloop")
call timer_print_summary(tmr, units="m", units_mod="kyr", time_mod=ts%time*1e-3)

title: timing
text:
timeout
timeout answers the question “should output be written at this timestep?” It builds, once at initialization, a list of target output times from a namelist group, and then timeout_check returns .TRUE. on any step whose time matches one of those targets (within a tolerance of 1e-6). This decouples the output schedule from the integration timestep, so a model can step at fine resolution but write 2D fields only at coarse, configurable intervals.

Public API
type timeout_class
    character(len=56)     :: method     ! "none", "const", "file", or "times"
    character(len=56)     :: label
    real(wp), allocatable :: times(:)   ! resolved list of output times
    logical               :: active     ! .FALSE. when method="none"
    ! plus optional helper fields (filename, filenames, vnms) for caller use
end type
! Build the output-time list from a namelist group:
call timeout_init(tm, filename, group, label, time_init, time_end)

  type(timeout_class), intent(INOUT) :: tm
  character(len=*),    intent(IN)    :: filename   ! namelist file
  character(len=*),    intent(IN)    :: group      ! namelist group name
  character(len=*),    intent(IN)    :: label      ! label for log messages
  real(wp),            intent(IN)    :: time_init  ! start of run
  real(wp),            intent(IN)    :: time_end   ! end of run
! Test whether the current time is an output time:
out_now = timeout_check(tm, time)

  type(timeout_class), intent(IN) :: tm
  real(wp),            intent(IN) :: time
  logical                         :: out_now
The namelist group is read for a method and its parameters:

method = "none" — output disabled; tm%active = .FALSE..
method = "const" — reads dt; emits time_init, time_init+dt, ... up to time_end.
method = "times" — reads an explicit times list; keeps those within [time_init, time_end].
method = "file" — reads file, an ASCII file of times; each line is either a single value or a t0:dt:t1 range that expands to a vector.

In every active case time_end is guaranteed to be included, and duplicate times are removed. The number of output times is capped at nmax = 100000.


Usage
type(timeout_class) :: tm_2D, tm_1D

! Configure two independent schedules from namelist groups "tm_2D" / "tm_1D":
call timeout_init(tm_2D, path_par, "tm_2D", "heavy", ts%time_init, ts%time_end)
call timeout_init(tm_1D, path_par, "tm_1D", "small", ts%time_init, ts%time_end)

do while (.not. ts%is_finished)
    call tstep_update(ts, dtt)
    ! ... model update ...

    if (tm_2D%active .and. timeout_check(tm_2D, ts%time)) then
        call domain_write_step(dom, outfldr, ts%time)   ! coarse 2D snapshots
    end if
    if (tm_1D%active .and. timeout_check(tm_1D, ts%time)) then
        call domain_write_1D(dom, outfldr, ts%time)      ! frequent 1D timeseries
    end if
end do
with a namelist group such as:
&tm_2D
    method = "const"    ! "none", "const", "file", "times"
    dt     = 1e3
    file   = "input/timeout_ramp_100kyr.txt"
    times  = 0
/

title: timing
text:
timestepping
timestepping owns the model’s time axis: it initializes the start/end times, advances the clock by dt each iteration, and tracks several parallel “time keepers” — an elapsed counter and calendar/relative times — using Kahan summation to avoid drift over many small steps. It also flags when the run is finished. It does not decide when to do anything (that is timeout); it only holds and advances the current time.

Public API
type tstep_class
    character(len=56) :: units         ! time units label
    character(len=56) :: method        ! "const"/"cal"/"rel" (see below)
    real(wp) :: time_init, time_end
    real(wp) :: time                   ! current output time (method-dependent)
    real(wp) :: time_elapsed           ! time since time_init
    real(wp) :: time_cal               ! calendar time
    real(wp) :: time_rel               ! relative time
    integer  :: n                      ! iteration counter
    logical  :: is_finished            ! .TRUE. once time >= time_end
    ! ... plus internal compensation/reference fields ...
end type
! Initialize the time axis:
call tstep_init(ts, time_init, time_end, method, units [, time_ref] [, const_cal] [, const_rel])

  type(tstep_class), intent(INOUT) :: ts
  real(wp),          intent(IN)    :: time_init, time_end
  character(len=*),  intent(IN)    :: method   ! see the three methods below
  character(len=*),  intent(IN)    :: units
  real(wp),          intent(IN)    :: time_ref    ! optional; reference for cal<->rel conversion
  real(wp),          intent(IN)    :: const_cal   ! optional; fixed calendar time
  real(wp),          intent(IN)    :: const_rel   ! optional; fixed relative time
The method selects which time keeper drives ts%time:

"const" / "constant" — time = time_init + time_elapsed; time_cal and time_rel are held fixed (a pure spin-up clock).
"cal" / "calendar" — time = time_cal evolves; time_rel = time_cal - time_ref.
"rel" / "relative" — time = time_rel evolves; time_cal = time_rel + time_ref.

! Advance one step by dt (updates all time keepers and is_finished):
call tstep_update(ts, dt [, verbose])

  type(tstep_class), intent(INOUT) :: ts
  real(wp),          intent(IN)    :: dt
  logical,           intent(IN)    :: verbose  ! optional; print a row each step

! Print a status row / header (iter, time, time_cal, time_rel, time_elapsed):
call tstep_print(ts)
call tstep_print_header(ts)
Note tstep_init requires time_end > time_init and stops otherwise. The first tstep_update only increments the counter (n); accumulation begins from the second call.


Usage
Drive the main loop off ts%is_finished, advancing at the top of each iteration:
type(tstep_class) :: ts

call tstep_init(ts, time_init, time_end, method="cal", time_ref=1950.0_wp, units="yr")

call tstep_print_header(ts)
do while (.not. ts%is_finished)
    call tstep_update(ts, dtt)
    call tstep_print(ts)

    ! ... perform model updates here, using ts%time / ts%time_rel / ts%time_cal ...
end do

See also

tsgen — time-series forcing generator, which consumes the ts%time produced here to drive a model’s boundary forcing.

title: root_finder
text:
root_finder locates a zero of a scalar function \(f(x)\), i.e. a value x for which f(x) = 0. Two iterative methods are provided:

Newton’s method (solve_newton) — uses the function f and its analytic derivative fp. From the current guess it steps along the tangent to that line’s x-intercept, giving quadratic convergence near a simple root.
Secant method (solve_secant) — a derivative-free fallback that approximates the slope from two successive iterates, so only f is needed. It is seeded internally from a single initial guess.







Figure 1: Newton’s method converging to a root: each iteration steps along the tangent at the current guess to its x-intercept.



Both routines share the same convergence controls. Iteration stops as soon as either the residual abs(f(x)) < tol or the step size falls below tol; the step-size test also catches flat roots (e.g. multiple roots) where the residual stays above tol even though x has effectively stopped moving. At most n_max iterations are taken. Each routine guards against its degenerate case — a zero derivative in Newton, equal successive function values in the secant — and exits without dividing by zero. The optional converged flag reports success; on failure a warning is written and converged is set .false..
Note: the tolerance argument was renamed eps → tol (see CHANGELOG).

title: root_finder
text:
root_finder locates a zero of a scalar function \(f(x)\), i.e. a value x for which f(x) = 0. Two iterative methods are provided:

Newton’s method (solve_newton) — uses the function f and its analytic derivative fp. From the current guess it steps along the tangent to that line’s x-intercept, giving quadratic convergence near a simple root.
Secant method (solve_secant) — a derivative-free fallback that approximates the slope from two successive iterates, so only f is needed. It is seeded internally from a single initial guess.







Figure 1: Newton’s method converging to a root: each iteration steps along the tangent at the current guess to its x-intercept.



Both routines share the same convergence controls. Iteration stops as soon as either the residual abs(f(x)) < tol or the step size falls below tol; the step-size test also catches flat roots (e.g. multiple roots) where the residual stays above tol even though x has effectively stopped moving. At most n_max iterations are taken. Each routine guards against its degenerate case — a zero derivative in Newton, equal successive function values in the secant — and exits without dividing by zero. The optional converged flag reports success; on failure a warning is written and converged is set .false..
Note: the tolerance argument was renamed eps → tol (see CHANGELOG).

title: root_finder
text:
Public API
The module exports two subroutines:
public :: solve_newton
public :: solve_secant

solve_newton
subroutine solve_newton(x, n_iter, x_init, tol, n_max, f, fp, debug, converged)

    real(wp), intent(OUT) :: x          ! Best guess of root
    integer,  intent(OUT) :: n_iter     ! Number of iterations taken
    real(wp), intent(IN)  :: x_init     ! Initial guess
    real(wp), intent(IN)  :: tol        ! Tolerance to convergence
    integer,  intent(IN)  :: n_max      ! Maximum iterations allowed
    real(wp), external    :: f          ! Function to find root of
    real(wp), external    :: fp         ! Derivative of the function
    logical,  intent(IN)  :: debug      ! Print iteration information?
    logical,  intent(OUT), optional :: converged  ! Did the method converge?
The functions f and fp are passed as external procedures, each taking a single real(wp) argument and returning a real(wp) value. x returns the best estimate of the root and n_iter the number of iterations used.


solve_secant
subroutine solve_secant(x, n_iter, x_init, tol, n_max, f, debug, converged)

    real(wp), intent(OUT) :: x          ! Best guess of root
    integer,  intent(OUT) :: n_iter     ! Number of iterations taken
    real(wp), intent(IN)  :: x_init     ! Initial guess
    real(wp), intent(IN)  :: tol        ! Tolerance to convergence
    integer,  intent(IN)  :: n_max      ! Maximum iterations allowed
    real(wp), external    :: f          ! Function to find root of
    logical,  intent(IN)  :: debug      ! Print iteration information?
    logical,  intent(OUT), optional :: converged  ! Did the method converge?
Identical to solve_newton but without the fp argument: the slope is estimated from two iterates. The second seed point is derived internally from x_init.

title: root_finder
text:
Usage example
Define the objective function and its derivative as external functions, then call solve_newton. This finds \(\sqrt{2}\) as the positive root of \(f(x) = x^2 - 2\):
program example_root
    use precision,   only: wp
    use root_finder, only: solve_newton

    implicit none

    real(wp) :: x
    integer  :: n_iter
    logical  :: converged

    call solve_newton(x, n_iter, x_init=1.5_wp, tol=1e-6_wp, n_max=100, &
                      f=f_sq, fp=fp_sq, debug=.false., converged=converged)

    if (converged) then
        write(*,*) "root = ", x, " after ", n_iter, " iterations"
    else
        write(*,*) "no convergence"
    end if

contains

    real(wp) function f_sq(x)
        real(wp) :: x
        f_sq = x*x - 2.0_wp
    end function f_sq

    real(wp) function fp_sq(x)
        real(wp) :: x
        fp_sq = 2.0_wp*x
    end function fp_sq

end program example_root
The derivative-free variant is called the same way, omitting fp:
call solve_secant(x, n_iter, x_init=2.0_wp, tol=1e-6_wp, n_max=100, &
                  f=f_sq, debug=.false., converged=converged)

title: ncio
text:
ncio is a high-level Fortran wrapper over the netCDF library (Robinson & Perrette). It reduces netCDF I/O to a handful of generic calls: create a file, define dimensions, and read or write variables and attributes without managing file IDs, variable IDs, dimension IDs, or data types by hand. The generic interfaces dispatch automatically on the rank (point through 6D) and type (integer, single, double, logical, character) of the array passed, and dimensions are referred to by name rather than by numeric ID.
The library is self-contained: it depends only on the netCDF Fortran library and standard intrinsics. All routines live in the ncio module (src/ncio.f90), with an optional companion module ncio_transpose (src/ncio_transpose.f90).

title: ncio
text:
ncio is a high-level Fortran wrapper over the netCDF library (Robinson & Perrette). It reduces netCDF I/O to a handful of generic calls: create a file, define dimensions, and read or write variables and attributes without managing file IDs, variable IDs, dimension IDs, or data types by hand. The generic interfaces dispatch automatically on the rank (point through 6D) and type (integer, single, double, logical, character) of the array passed, and dimensions are referred to by name rather than by numeric ID.
The library is self-contained: it depends only on the netCDF Fortran library and standard intrinsics. All routines live in the ncio module (src/ncio.f90), with an optional companion module ncio_transpose (src/ncio_transpose.f90).

title: ncio
text:
Core routines
All public entities are exported from module ncio. The main generic interfaces and subroutines are grouped below.

File

nc_create — create a new netCDF file, optionally with overwrite, netcdf4, and global metadata (author, creation_date, institution, description).
nc_open — open an existing file, returning ncid; optional writable flag.
nc_close — close an open file given its ncid.



Dimensions

nc_write_dim — define and write a coordinate dimension variable from its axis values x, with optional units, etc. (generic over integer/single/double, point or 1D).
nc_write_map — write a CRS / grid-mapping variable describing a projection.
nc_write_attr_std_dim — attach standard CF attributes to conventional dimension variables.



Variables

nc_write — write a variable of any supported type and rank (point–6D). Dimensions are given by name via dim1, dim2, … (or a dims(:) array); optional metadata includes long_name, standard_name, units, grid_mapping, missing_value, plus start/count for hyperslab writes.
nc_read — read a variable into an array of matching type and rank, with optional start, count, missing_value, ncid, iostat.



Attributes

nc_write_attr — write a variable-level or global attribute (character, integer, single, double; scalar or 1D vector).
nc_read_attr — read a variable-level or global attribute (same type coverage, including 1D vector attributes).



Queries

nc_size — return the length of a named dimension/variable.
nc_dims — return the dimension names and sizes of a variable.
nc_ndims — return the number of dimensions of a variable.
nc_exists_var — test whether a variable exists in a file.
nc_exists_attr — test whether an attribute exists (global or variable-level).
nc_size_attr — return the length of an attribute (global or variable-level).
nc_time_index — find the index of a given time value in a file’s time coordinate (single or double).

title: ncio
text:
Usage example
The canonical pattern is: create the file, write coordinate dimensions, write variables referencing those dimensions by name, then read data back.
use ncio

character(len=*), parameter :: filename = "output.nc"
real(8) :: time(100), f(100)
real(8) :: f_in(100)
integer :: i

do i = 1, 100
    time(i) = real(i, 8)
    f(i)    = sin(time(i))
end do

! Create the file and define the time dimension
call nc_create(filename)
call nc_write_dim(filename, "time", x=time, units="yr")

! Write a variable along the "time" dimension, with metadata
call nc_write(filename, "f", f, dim1="time", &
              long_name="Transient forcing value", units="1")

! Add a global attribute
call nc_write_attr(filename, "title", "ncio usage example")

! Read the variable back
call nc_read(filename, "f", f_in)
Multi-dimensional variables list additional dimensions with dim2, dim3, …:
call nc_write_dim(filename, "time",  x=time,  units="yr")
call nc_write_dim(filename, "month", x=month)
call nc_write(filename, "dTmon", dTmon, dim1="time", dim2="month")

title: ncio
section: Performance: reuse an open file with ncid
text:
Performance: reuse an open file with ncid
By default every nc_write / nc_read / nc_size call given a filename opens the file, performs the operation, and closes it. That is convenient for one-off calls, but when writing or reading many variables (or many time slices) to the same file the repeated open/close dominates the cost.
Open the file once with nc_open, thread the returned ncid through the calls, and close it with nc_close:
integer :: ncid, k

call nc_create(filename, netcdf4=.TRUE.)
call nc_write_dim(filename, "x",    x=x)
call nc_write_dim(filename, "time", x=[0.d0], unlimited=.TRUE.)

call nc_open(filename, ncid, writable=.TRUE.)
do k = 1, nt
    call nc_write(filename, "field", slice(:,:,k), dim1="x", dim2="time", &
                  start=[1, k], count=[nx, 1], ncid=ncid)
end do
call nc_close(ncid)
For a loop of many slice writes this is roughly 2–3× faster than the filename-per-call form and brings ncio to within ~2× of hand-written netCDF. Prefer it whenever more than a handful of operations touch the same file.

title: ncio
text:
ncio_transpose
module ncio_transpose provides the generic interfaces nc_write_t and nc_read_t for reading and writing arrays in transposed dimension order (2D and 3D, double precision). They wrap the ordinary nc_write / nc_read calls: the data is transposed in memory before writing (e.g. [j,i] to on-disk [i,j], [k,j,i] to [i,j,k]) and transposed back on read. This is convenient when the in-memory array layout differs from the desired on-disk axis ordering. All the usual optional arguments (dim1…dim6, dims, start, count, long_name, units, missing_value, etc.) are forwarded.

title: ncio
text:
Notes

Compression. netCDF4 output defaults to deflate level 1, lowering memory use for compressed files.
Error messages. I/O operations are threaded with a context (filename, variable, dimension, attribute) so that failures report exactly which file/variable/dimension/attribute led to the error.
Vector attributes. Both nc_write_attr and nc_read_attr support scalar and 1D (vector) numeric attributes for integer, single, and double types, at both variable and global level.
Generated source. The many per-type/per-rank wrapper routines behind nc_write, nc_read, nc_write_dim, and the attribute interfaces are generated from the fypp template src/ncio.fypp; src/ncio.f90 is the committed, generated output. Contributors editing those families edit the template and run make ncio-gen (fypp is vendored at tools/fypp.py; no Python is needed for an ordinary build). All other code lives directly in the template as pass-through Fortran.

title: gaussian_quadrature
text:
gaussian_quadrature provides the finite-element machinery for numerically integrating fields over structured grid cells treated as finite elements. The module is built around 3D hexahedral elements (8 nodes per element) and their 2D quadrilateral faces (4 nodes per element), the natural elements for a regular ice-sheet / ESM grid.
Numerical integration over an element is approximated by a weighted sum of the integrand evaluated at a small set of quadrature points. For these elements the module uses the standard 2×2×2 (3D) and 2×2 (2D) Gauss rule, with quadrature points located at \(\pm 1/\sqrt{3}\) in each direction of the reference element and unit weights. Each element carries trilinear (3D) or bilinear (2D) shape/basis functions N, together with their derivatives with respect to the reference coordinates, which map nodal (corner) values to values at the quadrature points and support Jacobian-based mapping to physical coordinates.
The reference hexahedron spans \(x,y,z \in (-1,1)\), indexed counter-clockwise from the SW corner with nodes 1–4 on the lower surface and 5–8 on the upper surface:
N4----N3        N8----N7
|     |  lower  |     |  upper
N1----N2        N5----N6
The intended use is finite-element integration and interpolation over ice-sheet model fields: staggering a variable from grid nodes to element corners, then evaluating it at the element’s quadrature points for integration.

title: gaussian_quadrature
text:
Public API
The module exports two derived types and their associated procedures:
public :: gq2D_class
public :: gq2D_init
public :: gq2D_to_nodes_aa
public :: gq2D_to_nodes_ab
public :: gq2D_to_nodes_acx
public :: gq2D_to_nodes_acy

public :: gq3D_class
public :: gq3D_init
public :: gq3D_to_nodes_aa
public :: gq3D_to_nodes_ab
public :: gq3D_to_nodes_acx
public :: gq3D_to_nodes_acy
public :: gq3D_to_nodes_acz

Derived types (element setup)
gq2D_class and gq3D_class hold the fixed reference-element geometry (filled once by the *_init routines) together with per-call working storage.
gq2D_class (4-node quadrilateral face):
type gq2D_class
    integer :: n_qp            ! number of quadrature points (4)
    integer :: n_nodes         ! number of nodes / corners (4)
    real(8) :: xr(4)           ! quadrature-point x in reference element
    real(8) :: yr(4)           ! quadrature-point y in reference element
    real(8) :: wt(4)           ! quadrature-point weights
    real(8) :: wt_tot          ! total weight (reference area = 4)
    real(8) :: N(4,4)          ! shape functions N(node, qp)
    real(8) :: dNdxr(4,4)      ! dN/dx in reference element
    real(8) :: dNdyr(4,4)      ! dN/dy in reference element
    real(8) :: v_ab(4)         ! variable value at cell corners
    real(8) :: dNdx(4)         ! at nodes
    real(8) :: dNdy(4)         ! at nodes
    real(8) :: detJ(4)         ! at nodes
    real(8) :: v(4)            ! variable value at quadrature points
end type
gq3D_class (8-node hexahedron) has the analogous fields extended to 8 nodes / quadrature points, plus the third dimension:
type gq3D_class
    integer :: n_qp            ! number of quadrature points (8)
    integer :: n_nodes         ! number of nodes / corners (8)
    real(8) :: xr(8), yr(8), zr(8)   ! quadrature-point reference coordinates
    real(8) :: wt(8)                 ! quadrature-point weights
    real(8) :: wt_tot                ! total weight (reference volume = 8)
    real(8) :: N(8,8)                ! shape functions N(node, qp)
    real(8) :: dNdxr(8,8)            ! dN/dx in reference element
    real(8) :: dNdyr(8,8)            ! dN/dy in reference element
    real(8) :: dNdzr(8,8)            ! dN/dz in reference element
    real(8) :: vol0                  ! volume scale
    real(8) :: v_ab(8)               ! variable value at cell corners
    real(8) :: dNdx(8), dNdy(8), dNdz(8)   ! at nodes
    real(8) :: detJ(8)               ! at nodes
    real(8) :: v(8)                  ! variable value at quadrature points
end type


Initialization (quadrature points and shape functions)
Each *_init routine populates the reference-element geometry: it sets n_qp and n_nodes, places the quadrature points at \(\pm 1/\sqrt{3}\), assigns the weights, and evaluates the shape functions N and their reference derivatives dNdxr, dNdyr (and dNdzr in 3D) at every quadrature point.
subroutine gq2D_init(gq, verbose)
    type(gq2D_class), intent(OUT)          :: gq
    logical,          intent(IN), optional :: verbose

subroutine gq3D_init(gq, verbose)
    type(gq3D_class), intent(OUT)          :: gq
    logical,          intent(IN), optional :: verbose
The optional verbose flag prints the computed basis functions and derivatives for each quadrature point (including checks that sum(N) = 1 and the derivative sums vanish).


Evaluation at quadrature points (staggering and interpolation)
The *_to_nodes_* routines stagger a gridded variable var to the element corners (gq%v_ab) and then evaluate it at the element’s quadrature points via the shape functions, returning the quadrature-point values in v_qp (and also storing them in gq%v). The suffix denotes the grid on which the input variable var lives:

_aa — cell-centred (aa) grid
_ab — corner (ab) grid
_acx — x-staggered (acx) grid
_acy — y-staggered (acy) grid
_acz — z-staggered (acz) grid (3D only)

2D (integration over a quadrilateral face):
subroutine gq2D_to_nodes_aa (gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, i, j, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1)
subroutine gq2D_to_nodes_ab (gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, i, j, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1)
subroutine gq2D_to_nodes_acx(gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, i, j, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1)
subroutine gq2D_to_nodes_acy(gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, i, j, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1)
with the common argument set:
    type(gq2D_class), intent(INOUT) :: gq          ! Gaussian Quadrature 2D object
    real(wp),         intent(INOUT) :: v_qp(4)     ! variable values at quadrature points
    real(wp),         intent(IN)    :: var(:,:)    ! variable to be interpolated
    real(wp),         intent(IN)    :: dx, dy      ! horizontal grid spacing
    integer,          intent(IN)    :: i, j        ! [x,y] indices of current cell
    integer,          intent(IN)    :: im1, ip1, jm1, jp1  ! neighbor indices
3D (integration over a hexahedral element):
subroutine gq3D_to_nodes_aa (gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, dz0, dz1, i, j, k, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1, km1, kp1)
subroutine gq3D_to_nodes_ab (gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, dz0, dz1, i, j, k, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1, km1, kp1)
subroutine gq3D_to_nodes_acx(gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, dz0, dz1, i, j, k, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1, km1, kp1)
subroutine gq3D_to_nodes_acy(gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, dz0, dz1, i, j, k, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1, km1, kp1)
subroutine gq3D_to_nodes_acz(gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, dz0, dz1, i, j, k, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1, km1, kp1)
with the common argument set:
    type(gq3D_class), intent(INOUT) :: gq          ! Gaussian Quadrature 3D object
    real(wp),         intent(INOUT) :: v_qp(8)     ! variable values at quadrature points
    real(wp),         intent(IN)    :: var(:,:,:)  ! variable to be interpolated
    real(wp),         intent(IN)    :: dx, dy      ! horizontal grid spacing (const)
    real(wp),         intent(IN)    :: dz0         ! dz to cell below
    real(wp),         intent(IN)    :: dz1         ! dz to cell above
    integer,          intent(IN)    :: i, j, k     ! [x,y,z] indices of current cell
    integer,          intent(IN)    :: im1, ip1, jm1, jp1, km1, kp1  ! neighbor indices
Here dz0 and dz1 are the vertical distances to the cell below and above, allowing non-uniform vertical spacing while the horizontal grid is assumed regular.

title: gaussian_quadrature
text:
Usage example
The typical pattern is: initialise an element once, then loop over grid cells, staggering the field to corners and evaluating it at the quadrature points. The quadrature-point values in v_qp (weighted by gq%wt) are then used to approximate the element integral.
use gaussian_quadrature

type(gq3D_class) :: gq
real(wp) :: v_qp(8)
real(wp) :: var(nx,ny,nz)      ! field on the aa (cell-centred) grid
real(wp) :: dx, dy, dz0, dz1
integer  :: i, j, k
real(wp) :: elem_integral

! 1. Set up the reference hexahedron: quadrature points, weights, shape functions
call gq3D_init(gq)

! 2. For a given cell (i,j,k), evaluate var at the element's quadrature points.
!    Neighbor indices im1/ip1/... would normally respect the model's
!    boundary conditions; interior points are shown here.
call gq3D_to_nodes_aa(gq, v_qp, var, dx, dy, dz0, dz1, &
                      i, j, k, i-1, i+1, j-1, j+1, k-1, k+1)

! 3. Approximate the element integral as a weighted sum over quadrature points.
!    (For a full integral, multiply by the Jacobian determinant gq%detJ.)
elem_integral = sum(gq%wt(1:gq%n_qp) * v_qp(1:gq%n_qp))
The 2D routines follow the same pattern with gq2D_class, gq2D_init, the gq2D_to_nodes_* calls, and 4-element arrays, integrating over a quadrilateral face instead of a hexahedron.

title: coords
text:
coords is the geometry and remapping core of fesm-utils. It is a clean rebuild of the older coordinates library, keeping the user-facing API (points_class / grid_class / map_class, *_init, map_field) while replacing the internal storage, neighbour search and conservative remapping with a single design. Everything is reached through one umbrella module:
use coords
The package covers:

Coordinate and grid definition — point sets (points_class) and regular grids (grid_class), each carrying a shared coordinate-system descriptor (coord_system: map type, units, planet, projection).
Map projections — the oblimap2 oblique stereographic forward/inverse transforms, driven by projection parameters (lambda, phi, alpha, x_e, y_n).
Gaussian-latitude grids — quadrature latitudes for spectral/atmospheric grids (non-uniform y-axis).
k-d tree nearest-neighbour search — the fast neighbour engine underneath distance-based maps.
Weight-map remapping — one sparse source→target store applied with nearest-neighbour, Shepard (inverse-distance), quadrant, bilinear or conservative (area-weighted) kernels.
SCRIP-superset weight-map file I/O — maps are cached as valid SCRIP remapping files, interoperable with cdo and other tools.
CDO grid-description interop and cached map generation — read/write cdo griddes files, generate weights online (in-package or via an external cdo call), and load-if-exists / save-on-generate caching.
The multigrid coupler — a thin registry + lazy map-cache layer for remapping fields between many named grids.

title: coords
text:
Key derived types
All types are defined in coordinates.f90 (geometry) and mapping.f90 (maps), and re-exported by use coords.







Type
Role




coord_system
Coordinate-reference metadata (mtype, units, planet, proj, xy_conv, is_cartesian, is_projection, is_lon180). Defined once and embedded as %cs in every geometry type.


points_class
An unstructured set of points: npts, 1D arrays x, y, lon, lat, area, and %cs.


grid_axis_class
Regular axis description: nx, ny, x0, dx, y0, dy and the axis vectors x(:), y(:) (supports explicit non-uniform axes, e.g. Gaussian latitudes).


grid_class
A regular grid: name, axis block %G (grid_axis_class), 2D arrays x, y, lon, lat, area, and %cs.


map_class
Public map wrapper: source/target names (name1, name2), target coordinate system %cs, target axes %G, fixed interpolation method, and the internal weight store %wm.


weight_map_t
The single sparse source→target store (link-centric, SCRIP-style). Usually accessed only through map_class; see below.


coupler_class
A registry of named grids plus a lazily-built cache of grid→grid maps (coupler.f90).




The weight store
weight_map_t (module weight_map) is one sparse matrix with two payload flavours, selected by %kind:

MAP_DISTANCE — links carry the neighbour distance, position (xn, yn) and quadrant; the per-link weight is built at apply time from the chosen kernel. One cached distance map therefore serves nn / quadrant / shepard / bilinear and adapts to a time-varying source mask.
MAP_WEIGHT — links carry a final baked weight w(:); apply just reduces it (conservative maps, imported SCRIP maps, or precomputed bilinear).

Links are grouped by destination; dst_off(:) is a CSR offset array. The type is rarely handled directly — map_init / map_field wrap it — but the low-level API (weight_map_alloc, weight_map_index, weight_map_apply, weight_map_prune, weight_map_free) is public for building custom maps.

title: coords
text:
Core workflow
The typical use is: define a source and target grid, generate (or load) a weight map between them, then apply that map to remap one or more fields.
use coords

type(grid_class) :: gsrc, gtgt
type(map_class)  :: map
real(dp), allocatable :: field_src(:,:), field_tgt(:,:)

! 1. Define source and target grids (here two Cartesian grids in km).
call grid_init(gsrc, name="src", mtype="cartesian", units="kilometers", &
               x0=0.0_dp,   dx=100.0_dp, nx=10, y0=0.0_dp,   dy=100.0_dp, ny=10)
call grid_init(gtgt, name="tgt", mtype="cartesian", units="kilometers", &
               x0=150.0_dp, dx=100.0_dp, nx=7,  y0=150.0_dp, dy=100.0_dp, ny=7)

! 2. Generate (or load) a weight map. `method` fixes the kernel; the map is
!    cached under `fldr` and reloaded next time when load=.true. (the default).
call map_init(map, gsrc, gtgt, method="shepard", max_neighbors=8, fldr="maps")

! 3. Apply it. var1 is the source field; var2 is sized to the target grid.
allocate(field_src(10,10), field_tgt(7,7))
! ... fill field_src ...
call map_field(map, "myfield", field_src, field_tgt, stat="mean")
grid_init is generic and also accepts explicit axis vectors (x=, y=), an existing grid/points object, or a file (grid_init(grid, filename, ...) reads a .nc grid or a /map/ namelist). Point sets are built the same way with points_init(pts, name=, mtype=, units=, x=, y=, ...).
map_init is overloaded for grid→grid, grid→points, points→grid, points→points, and grid→grid by name (map_init(map, name1, name2, ...), which reads both grid descriptions from grid_<name>.txt). Key optional arguments to the grid→grid form:

method — interpolation kernel: "con" (conservative) or a distance kernel "nn" / "shepard" / "quadrant" / "bilin" (default "nn").
gen — generator used when weights must actually be built: "coords" (in-package, default) or "cdo" (external cdo system call).
fldr — map cache directory (default "maps").
load — if .true. (default) an existing cache file is loaded instead of regenerated; freshly generated maps are always saved.
max_neighbors, dist_max — neighbour-search controls for distance maps.
precompute_weights — collapse a distance store to baked weights at init for shepard/bilinear (default .true.); the on-disk cache stays distance-format either way.
weight_cutoff — relative cutoff for pruning sliver links from baked (con/bilinear) maps before caching (default 1e-4; 0.0 keeps all links).

map_field is generic over the field element type (integer, sp, dp) and over grid/points sources and targets. Beyond stat it accepts method (kernel override for distance maps), missing_value, radius, mask2, reset, mask_pack, and optional fill/filter post-processing (fill_method, filt_method, filt_par). The stat argument selects the reduction over each target’s links: "mean" (default), "count" (weighted mode), or "stdev".

title: coords
text:
Remapping kernels
The kernel is fixed when the map is generated (it is part of the cache key) and selected via method:

nn (nearest) — copies the single nearest valid source value. Use for categorical/index fields, masks, and where no smoothing is wanted.
shepard — inverse-distance-squared weighting over the neighbours within radius. A good general-purpose smooth interpolation for scattered or point-set sources.
quadrant — nearest valid neighbour in each of the four quadrants, IDW combined; more isotropic than plain Shepard near data gaps.
bilin (bilinear) — for structured source→target pairs, exact index-space bilinear interpolation (matches cdo genbil). Best for smooth fields on regular grids; bakes to a MAP_WEIGHT map.
con (conservative) — area-weighted overlap via analytic polygon clipping. Conserves the area integral, so it is the right choice for fluxes and for aggregating a fine grid onto a coarse one. Built as a MAP_WEIGHT map.

Distance kernels (nn/shepard/quadrant) share a single MAP_DISTANCE store, so the kernel can also be overridden per call in map_field(method=) without rebuilding the map. Conservative and bilinear are baked weights and are fixed at generation.

title: coords
text:
CDO interop and map caching
Grids round-trip through cdo griddes text files (module grid_cdo):

grid_cdo_write_desc_short(grid, fldr) writes grid_<name>.txt, a cdo-compliant description with a #-comment header carrying the full coord_system so the grid can be reconstructed exactly.
grid_cdo_read_desc(grid, name, fldr) rebuilds a grid_class from such a file (the reader falls back on the CF grid_mapping_name for files stripped of the fesm-utils header, e.g. native cdo descriptions).

Map caching is handled inside map_init. The cache filename is derived from the two grid names, the folder and the method (gen_map_filename), and:

if load=.true. (default) and the file exists, the map is loaded, with the file format auto-detected — an in-package cache (carries a meta variable) vs a standard/cdo SCRIP file;
otherwise the weights are generated (in-package for gen="coords", or by an external cdo gen<method> call for gen="cdo") and saved to the cache.

Because the cache is always a valid SCRIP remapping file, a cdo-generated map and an in-package coords map interoperate at the same filename. The file I/O primitives are public as weight_map_write / weight_map_read (module map_io); map_read(map, src_name, dst_name, fldr, method) loads a pre-existing SCRIP map directly into a map_class.

title: coords
text:
Coupler
The coupler module (module coupler, use coupler) sits on top of the mapping core and manages many grids at once. A coupler_class holds a registry of named grids and a lazily populated cache of grid→grid maps: grids are nodes, maps are directed, method-typed edges.
use coords
use coupler

type(coupler_class)   :: cpl
type(grid_class)      :: gcoarse, gfine
real(dp), allocatable :: fs(:,:), ft(:,:)

call coupler_init(cpl, map_fldr="maps")          ! empty registry + cache
call coupler_add_grid(cpl, "coarse", gcoarse)    ! register grids by name
call coupler_add_grid(cpl, "fine",   gfine)

! remap sizes the destination itself; the (src,dst,method) map is built on first
! use and reused thereafter. method defaults to "con".
call remap(cpl, fs, "coarse", ft, "fine", method="bilin")

coupler_add_grid(cpl, name, grid) registers a grid; unregistered names are resolved on demand from grid_<name>.txt in the map folder.
remap(cpl, var_src, src, var_dst, dst, method, stat) finds-or-builds the map for (src, dst, method), allocates var_dst to the target size, and applies it. It is overloaded for dp / sp / int and 2D / 3D fields. method is part of the cache key; stat is forwarded per call and is not.
coupler_prime(cpl, src, dst, method, gen) eagerly builds a map up front (fail-fast at init rather than mid-timestep) and is the point at which a map’s generator can be overridden via gen=.

Each map is built once, stored once in fixed-capacity storage, and shared across all callers, so adding a map never reallocates or deep-copies the large weight arrays already built.

title: LIS
text:
LIS (Library of Iterative Solvers) is a parallel C/Fortran library for solving large, sparse systems of linear equations and eigenvalue problems with iterative methods (a wide range of Krylov-subspace solvers and preconditioners, including algebraic multigrid).
Version vendored: 2.1.11 (lis/lis-2.1.11/).

title: LIS
text:
Role in fesm-utils
LIS provides the sparse iterative linear solvers used by downstream Earth system models. It is built with the Fortran 90 interface and the SA-AMG (smoothed aggregation algebraic multigrid) preconditioner enabled.

title: LIS
text:
Building
LIS is built by its own autotools through the root Makefile:
make lis openmp=0        # serial -> lis/lis-serial
make lis openmp=1        # OpenMP -> lis/lis-omp
The target runs, in lis/lis-2.1.11/:
./configure --prefix=<repo>/lis/lis-<variant> --enable-f90 --enable-saamg [--enable-omp]
make && make install
The serial and OpenMP variants install side by side into lis/lis-serial and lis/lis-omp. Machine/compiler specifics (compiler pick, C-standard pins, Intel runtime-library fixes) live in config/libs.mk; the vendored 2.1.11 autotools were modernized to build cleanly with recent icx/ifx.

title: LIS
text:
Using it downstream
Reference the install prefix directly:
fesm-utils/lis/lis-serial/include     # lis.h, lisf.h, the lis Fortran module
fesm-utils/lis/lis-serial/lib         # liblis.a

title: LIS
text:
Updating the vendored source
To try a different upstream version, unpack it into the lis/ container folder and rebuild:
wget https://www.ssisc.org/lis/dl/lis-2.1.11.zip
unzip lis-2.1.11.zip -d lis/
rm lis-2.1.11.zip
make lis openmp=0

title: fesm-utils
text:
fesm-utils is a convenience repository holding a self-contained Fortran utility library together with the external C/Fortran libraries needed to build and run fast Earth system models such as CLIMBER-X and yelmox.
Everything is driven from a single root Makefile, generated per machine by configme. A serial and an OpenMP variant build side by side (openmp=0 / openmp=1).

title: fesm-utils
text:
What’s inside
The repository bundles the local utils library (src/, compiled into libfesmutils.a) plus three vendored external libraries, each in its own container folder with the upstream source and compiled serial/omp install prefixes.

Local packages



coords
Coordinate geometry, map projections, Gaussian grids, kdtree, and grid-to-grid weight-map remapping (nearest-neighbour / bilinear / conservative) with CDO interop and map caching.


tsgen
Transient scalar forcing from time (ramps, sinusoids), tabulated files, or model-response feedback controllers.


series
Lightweight tabulated time-series reader (ASCII/netCDF), scalar or multi-channel, with clamped-linear interpolation.


varslice
Slice gridded (x-y-z-time) variables out of netCDF files, with sub-annual axes, multi-file matching, and unit scaling.


ncio
High-level netCDF input/output for Fortran — create files, dimensions, variables, and attributes with minimal boilerplate.


nml
Read and write Fortran namelist parameter files, with a defaults overlay and validation.


root_finder
Scalar root finding via Newton and secant iterations.


timing
Wall-clock timers, output-write scheduling, and timestep management.


grid operations
Spatial derivatives, distances, and subgrid interpolation — plus Arakawa-grid staggering (work in progress).


gaussian_quadrature
Finite-element Gaussian quadrature over 3D hexahedral elements.


foundations
Precision kinds (wp/sp/dp) and package-wide constants every module builds on.



External libraries
Three vendored upstream libraries, each built by its own autotools configure/make/make install into <lib>/<lib>-{serial,omp}:

FFTW — the Fastest Fourier Transform in the West.
LIS — Library of Iterative Solvers for linear systems.
SHTns — fast Spherical Harmonic Transforms.

title: fesm-utils
text:
Installation
fesm-utils is built through configme, which is the single source of machine/compiler truth (Fortran flags, netCDF autodetection) and generates the root Makefile.

1. Install configme
pip install configme      # or: pipx install configme


2. Install fesm-utils
Clone, configure, and build in one step — select your machine and compiler:
configme install fesm-utils -m macbook -c gfortran
In an existing checkout, just (re)generate the Makefile:
configme config fesm-utils -m macbook -c gfortran


3. Build with make
One consistent interface, selecting the variant with openmp=:
make fesmutils-static openmp=0    # the utils library -> include-serial/libfesmutils.a
make fftw   openmp=0              # serial FFTW  -> fftw/fftw-serial
make lis    openmp=1             # OpenMP LIS   -> lis/lis-omp
make shtns  openmp=0             # serial SHTns -> SHTns/shtns-serial
make libs   openmp=0             # fftw + lis + shtns, one variant
make usage                       # list the main targets






Tip



Build the external libraries serially (a one-time autotools build — do not pass -j to make libs). SHTns links the FFTW built here, so fftw must be installed before shtns. On clusters, module load your compiler + netCDF toolchain first — the builds and netCDF autodetection use the environment already loaded in your shell.

title: fesm-utils
text:
Using the libraries downstream
Symlink the repository into a downstream model and reference the install prefixes directly:
cd climber-x/
ln -s /path/to/fesm-utils ./

utils: fesm-utils/include-serial (or include-omp)
FFTW: fesm-utils/fftw/fftw-serial
LIS: fesm-utils/lis/lis-serial
SHTns: fesm-utils/SHTns/shtns-serial

title: FFTW
text:
FFTW is a C library for computing discrete Fourier transforms in one or more dimensions, of arbitrary input size, in both real and complex data. It is a mature, widely used, high-performance FFT implementation.
Version vendored: 3.3.10 (fftw/fftw-3.3.10/).

title: FFTW
text:
Role in fesm-utils
FFTW is a build-time dependency of downstream models and of SHTns, which links against the FFTW built here for its spherical-harmonic transforms. Because of that link dependency, FFTW must be installed before SHTns.

title: FFTW
text:
Building
FFTW is built by its own autotools through the root Makefile:
make fftw openmp=0        # serial -> fftw/fftw-serial
make fftw openmp=1        # OpenMP -> fftw/fftw-omp
The target runs, in fftw/fftw-3.3.10/:
./configure --prefix=<repo>/fftw/fftw-<variant> --disable-doc [--enable-openmp]
make && make install
The serial and OpenMP variants install side by side into fftw/fftw-serial and fftw/fftw-omp. Machine/compiler specifics (the C compiler pick, C-standard pins) live in config/libs.mk.

title: FFTW
text:
Using it downstream
Reference the install prefix directly:
fesm-utils/fftw/fftw-serial/include    # fftw3.h, fftw3.f03
fesm-utils/fftw/fftw-serial/lib        # libfftw3.a (+ libfftw3_omp for the omp variant)

title: FFTW
text:
Updating the vendored source
To try a different upstream version, unpack it into the fftw/ container folder and rebuild:
wget https://www.fftw.org/fftw-3.3.10.tar.gz
tar -xvf fftw-3.3.10.tar.gz -C fftw/
rm fftw-3.3.10.tar.gz
make fftw openmp=0

title: SHTns
text:
SHTns is a high-performance C library for Spherical Harmonic Transforms — the forward and inverse transforms between values on a sphere and their spherical-harmonic coefficients. It provides a Fortran interface and is optimized with vectorized kernels.
Version vendored: 3.7.5 (SHTns/shtns-3.7.5/).

title: SHTns
text:
Role in fesm-utils
SHTns supplies the spherical-harmonic machinery used by downstream models (e.g. spectral transforms on the sphere). It links against the FFTW built in this repository, so FFTW must be installed first.

title: SHTns
text:
Building
SHTns is built by its own autotools through the root Makefile:
make fftw  openmp=0        # prerequisite: build FFTW first
make shtns openmp=0        # serial -> SHTns/shtns-serial
make shtns openmp=1        # OpenMP -> SHTns/shtns-omp
The target runs, in SHTns/shtns-3.7.5/, pointing SHTns at the FFTW prefix:
./configure --prefix=<repo>/SHTns/shtns-<variant> [--enable-openmp] \
    CPPFLAGS='-I<repo>/fftw/fftw-<variant>/include' \
    LDFLAGS='-L<repo>/fftw/fftw-<variant>/lib'
make && make install
The serial and OpenMP variants install side by side into SHTns/shtns-serial and SHTns/shtns-omp. Machine/compiler specifics — the kernel-compiler choice and -march handling — live in config/libs.mk.

title: SHTns
text:
Using it downstream
Reference the install prefix directly:
fesm-utils/SHTns/shtns-serial/include    # shtns.h, shtns.f03
fesm-utils/SHTns/shtns-serial/lib        # libshtns.a

title: SHTns
text:
Notes

SHTns is the last of the three external libraries to build, because of its FFTW link dependency. make libs builds fftw, lis, shtns in that order.
Build the external libraries serially (do not pass -j to make libs).

title: foundations
text:
The precision and constants modules are the shared base that every other fesm-utils module builds on. precision defines the real kinds used throughout the library — the working precision wp, plus explicit single (sp) and double (dp) kinds. constants defines the package-wide domain values: the missing value, error sentinels, mathematical constants, and tolerances.
The two concerns are kept separate on purpose. Floating-point kinds live in precision; domain values live in constants. As the constants header notes, precision kinds must be sourced directly from precision — they are not re-exported from constants.

title: foundations
text:
The precision and constants modules are the shared base that every other fesm-utils module builds on. precision defines the real kinds used throughout the library — the working precision wp, plus explicit single (sp) and double (dp) kinds. constants defines the package-wide domain values: the missing value, error sentinels, mathematical constants, and tolerances.
The two concerns are kept separate on purpose. Floating-point kinds live in precision; domain values live in constants. As the constants header notes, precision kinds must be sourced directly from precision — they are not re-exported from constants.

title: foundations
text:
precision
precision defines three integer kind parameters:



Name
Definition
Meaning




dp
kind(1.d0)
Double-precision real kind


sp
kind(1.0)
Single-precision real kind


wp
sp
Working precision (the default kind for library reals)



The working precision wp is selected by aliasing one of the explicit kinds. It is currently set to single precision (wp = sp); changing that one line retargets the working precision of the whole library.
use precision, only: wp

real(wp) :: x
Use sp or dp directly when a routine must pin a variable to a specific precision regardless of the working precision.

title: foundations
text:
constants
constants uses wp and dp from precision for its own declarations but keeps them private, so they are not re-exported — source kinds from precision. Values default to working precision (wp) except where a double-precision form is explicitly required (geometry, weights, and other double-only internals).







Name
Meaning




mv
Missing value, working precision (-9999.0_wp)


mv_dp
Missing value, double precision (-9999.0_dp)


ERR_DIST
Error distance sentinel — a very large distance (1E8_dp)


ERR_IND
Error index sentinel (-1)


pi
Pi, computed as 2*acos(0) in double precision


degrees_to_radians
Conversion factor pi / 180


radians_to_degrees
Conversion factor 180 / pi


TOL
General tolerance (1e-8_wp)


TOL_UNDERFLOW
Underflow tolerance (1e-15, cast to wp)



The missing value mv (-9999.0) is the library-wide flag for absent or invalid data, with mv_dp supplying the same sentinel at double precision for the double-only internals. The error sentinels ERR_DIST and ERR_IND mark failed distance and index lookups: ERR_DIST is a deliberately huge distance (1E8) so that a bad match never wins a nearest-neighbour comparison, and ERR_IND = -1 is an out-of-range index.
The mathematical constants are all double precision. pi is computed as 2*acos(0) rather than hard-coded, and degrees_to_radians / radians_to_degrees are derived from it for angle conversions. The tolerances TOL (1e-8) and TOL_UNDERFLOW (1e-15) are working precision; TOL is the general comparison tolerance and TOL_UNDERFLOW guards against underflow.

title: foundations
text:
Usage note
A module that needs both the working kind and the domain constants pulls each from its own module:
use precision, only: wp
use constants, only: mv, pi, TOL

real(wp) :: value

if (abs(value - mv) < TOL) then
    ! value is the missing-value flag
end if

title: grid operations
text: The grid-operations utilities collect the low-level array kernels used to work with fields defined on a regular 2D (and, for derivatives, 1D) grid: moving quantities between the nodes of an Arakawa staggered grid, taking spatial derivatives, measuring distances to a masked feature, and refining a coarse cell onto a finer subgrid. All routines operate on plain Fortran arrays and take the working precision wp from the precision module (except subgrid, which computes internally in double precision — see below).

title: grid operations
text:
staggering






WarningWork in progress — not yet callable



staggering is a stub and is not used by any other unit. The module declares private and has no public :: list, so none of the routines below are exported; additionally calc_magnitude_from_staggered calls get_neighbor_indices, which is neither defined in the module nor imported via use.
What follows documents the intended API. Before the module can be wired in it needs a public :: list and a boundaries-aware neighbour-index routine.


The staggering module moves fields between the nodes of an Arakawa C-grid. Scalar (cell-centred) quantities live on the aa-nodes; the two components of a vector live on the staggered ac-nodes at the cell edges (acx on the x-edge, acy on the y-edge); corner values live on the ab-nodes. Each procedure is a function of one or more real(wp) arrays that returns a new real(wp) array of the same shape. Averaging kernels apply periodic boundary conditions at the domain edges. Two *_ice variants restrict the averaging to neighbours that satisfy an ice mask (f_ice, H_ice), falling back to the plain four-point average when no masked neighbour is available.






Figure 1: Arakawa C-grid staggering: scalar quantities live on the centred aa-nodes; vector components live on the staggered ac-nodes at the cell edges.



Procedures (all real(wp)):
! Centred magnitude of a vector from its staggered (ac-node) components,
! restricted to fully ice-covered cells (f_ice == 1); `boundaries` selects the
! neighbour-indexing scheme.
function calc_magnitude_from_staggered(u, v, f_ice, boundaries) result(umag)
    real(wp), intent(IN) :: u(:,:), v(:,:)   ! acx-, acy-node components
    real(wp), intent(IN) :: f_ice(:,:)       ! ice-fraction mask
    character(len=*), intent(IN) :: boundaries
    real(wp) :: umag(:,:)                     ! aa-nodes

! Centred (aa-node) average of the staggered ac-node components.
function stagger_ac_aa(u, v) result(umag)    ! u: acx, v: acy  -> aa

! Corner/centre four-point averages between aa- and ab-nodes.
function stagger_aa_ab(u)     result(ustag)  ! aa -> ab
function stagger_ab_aa(u)     result(ustag)  ! ab -> aa
function stagger_aa_ab_ice(u, H_ice, f_ice) result(ustag)  ! aa -> ab, masked
function stagger_ab_aa_ice(u, H_ice, f_ice) result(ustag)  ! ab -> aa, masked

! Edge averages between aa/ab and the staggered ac-nodes.
function stagger_aa_acx(u) result(ustag)     ! aa  -> acx
function stagger_aa_acy(u) result(ustag)     ! aa  -> acy
function stagger_acx_aa(u) result(ustag)     ! acx -> aa
function stagger_acy_aa(u) result(ustag)     ! acy -> aa
function stagger_ab_acx(u) result(ustag)     ! ab  -> acx
function stagger_ab_acy(u) result(ustag)     ! ab  -> acy
Once the routines are exported, typical usage would be to interpolate cell-centred data onto the x- and y-edges, or recover a centred vector magnitude from staggered velocity components. (This does not compile against the module as it currently stands — see the note above.)
use precision,  only: wp
use staggering, only: stagger_aa_acx, stagger_aa_acy, stagger_ac_aa

real(wp) :: H(nx,ny)              ! aa-node scalar (e.g. ice thickness)
real(wp) :: ux(nx,ny), uy(nx,ny)  ! ac-node velocity components
real(wp) :: H_acx(nx,ny), H_acy(nx,ny)
real(wp) :: umag(nx,ny)

! Move the scalar field onto the staggered edges
H_acx = stagger_aa_acx(H)
H_acy = stagger_aa_acy(H)

! Recover the centred speed from the staggered velocity components
umag = stagger_ac_aa(ux, uy)

title: grid operations
text:
derivatives
The derivatives module computes first spatial derivatives of a gridded field using three-point finite-difference stencils. Interior points use a centred stencil; boundary points use a one-sided (upstream/downstream) stencil unless bc == "periodic", in which case the centred stencil wraps around. Derivatives are evaluated only where the logical mask is .true.; an optional lim argument clamps the result to [-lim, lim]. Stencil weights are accumulated in double precision internally.
Public procedures:
public :: calc_dvdx_2D
public :: calc_dvdy_2D
public :: calc_dvdx_1D
public :: calc_dvdy_1D

! 2D field: x- and y-derivatives
subroutine calc_dvdx_2D(dvdx, v, dx, mask, bc, lim)
    real(wp), intent(OUT) :: dvdx(:,:)
    real(wp), intent(IN)  :: v(:,:)
    real(wp), intent(IN)  :: dx
    logical,  intent(IN)  :: mask(:,:)
    character(len=*), intent(IN) :: bc      ! "periodic" or otherwise
    real(wp), intent(IN), optional :: lim   ! clamp |dvdx| <= lim

subroutine calc_dvdy_2D(dvdy, v, dy, mask, bc, lim)   ! same shape, y-direction

! 1D field
subroutine calc_dvdx_1D(dvdx, v, dx, mask, bc, lim)
    real(wp), intent(OUT) :: dvdx(:)
    real(wp), intent(IN)  :: v(:)
    real(wp), intent(IN)  :: dx
    logical,  intent(IN)  :: mask(:)
    character(len=*), intent(IN) :: bc
    real(wp), intent(IN), optional :: lim

subroutine calc_dvdy_1D(dvdy, v, dy, mask, bc)   ! delegates to calc_dvdx_1D
Note that calc_dvdy_1D has no lim argument: in 1D it simply forwards to calc_dvdx_1D (there dvdy == dvdx).
Example — gradient of a 2D field over its whole domain with a periodic x-boundary and a slope limit:
use precision,   only: wp
use derivatives, only: calc_dvdx_2D, calc_dvdy_2D

real(wp) :: z(nx,ny)            ! field to differentiate
real(wp) :: dzdx(nx,ny), dzdy(nx,ny)
logical  :: mask(nx,ny)

mask = .true.                   ! evaluate everywhere
call calc_dvdx_2D(dzdx, z, dx, mask, bc="periodic", lim=0.5_wp)
call calc_dvdy_2D(dzdy, z, dy, mask, bc="infinite")

title: grid operations
text:
distances
The distances module measures, for every grid cell, the distance to the nearest cell of a reference feature marked in an integer mask. It uses a chamfer (weighted grid-propagation) distance transform with horizontal/vertical weight 1, diagonal weight √2, and a knight-move term √5 for accuracy. The mask convention is -1 inside the feature, 0 on the feature (distance zero), and 1 outside; the returned distance is signed accordingly (negative inside). Distances are propagated in double precision.
Public procedure:
public :: compute_distance_to_mask

subroutine compute_distance_to_mask(dist, mask, periodic_x, periodic_y)
    real(wp), intent(OUT) :: dist(:,:)
    integer,  intent(IN)  :: mask(:,:)     ! -1 inside, 0 at feature, 1 outside
    logical,  intent(IN), optional :: periodic_x   ! wrap i-neighbours
    logical,  intent(IN), optional :: periodic_y   ! wrap j-neighbours
When neither axis is periodic (the default), a single forward+backward chamfer sweep is used. If either periodic_x or periodic_y is set, the forward and backward sweeps are iterated (with wrapped neighbour indices) until the distance field stops changing, so information can cross the periodic seam.
Example — signed distance to a coastline, wrapping in x:
use precision, only: wp
use distances, only: compute_distance_to_mask

integer  :: mask(nx,ny)        ! -1 land interior, 0 coast, 1 ocean
real(wp) :: dist(nx,ny)

call compute_distance_to_mask(dist, mask, periodic_x=.true.)
! dist < 0 inland, 0 on the coast, > 0 offshore (in grid-cell units)

title: grid operations
text:
subgrid
The subgrid module refines a single coarse cell of a 2D field onto an nxi × nxi subgrid by bilinear interpolation. It first reconstructs the four cell corners (ab-nodes) from the surrounding aa-node values, then fills the subgrid array with bilinearly interpolated values between those corners. When nxi == 1 the routine returns the cell-centre value unchanged.
As described in the source header, the interpolation is always performed in double precision: the *_dp procedures do the real work, and the single-precision path consists of thin wrappers that promote their real(sp) arguments, call the *_dp worker, and demote the result back to real(sp). Each operation is exposed through a generic interface so a caller built with wp = sp or wp = dp can use the generic name and have it resolve to the matching specific by argument kind.
Public procedures:
public :: calc_subgrid_array            ! generic (sp/dp)
public :: calc_subgrid_array_mask       ! generic (sp/dp), with mask
public :: calc_subgrid_array_cell       ! generic (sp/dp)
public :: calc_subgrid_array_dp         ! double-precision worker
public :: calc_subgrid_array_mask_dp    ! double-precision worker (mask)
public :: calc_subgrid_array_cell_dp    ! double-precision worker

! Generic name calc_subgrid_array resolves to calc_subgrid_array_{sp,dp}:
subroutine calc_subgrid_array_dp(vint, v, nxi, i, j, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1)
    real(dp), intent(INOUT) :: vint(:,:)          ! nxi x nxi output
    real(dp), intent(IN)    :: v(:,:)             ! full aa-node field
    integer,  intent(IN)    :: nxi                ! subgrid points per side
    integer,  intent(IN)    :: i, j               ! current cell indices
    integer,  intent(IN)    :: im1, ip1, jm1, jp1 ! neighbour indices

! Masked variant: corner averages only include neighbours where mask is true.
subroutine calc_subgrid_array_mask_dp(vint, v, mask, nxi, i, j, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1)
    real(dp), intent(INOUT) :: vint(:,:)
    real(dp), intent(IN)    :: v(:,:)
    logical,  intent(IN)    :: mask(:,:)
    integer,  intent(IN)    :: nxi
    integer,  intent(IN)    :: i, j, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1

! Interpolate a cell directly from its four corner values (quadrants 1..4).
subroutine calc_subgrid_array_cell_dp(vint, v1, v2, v3, v4, nxi)
    real(dp), intent(INOUT) :: vint(:,:)
    real(dp), intent(IN)    :: v1, v2, v3, v4     ! v2--v1 / v3--v4
    integer,  intent(IN)    :: nxi
The *_sp specifics (calc_subgrid_array_sp, calc_subgrid_array_mask_sp, calc_subgrid_array_cell_sp) have identical argument lists with real(sp) array/scalar arguments and are reached through the generic names rather than being called directly.
Example — refine each cell of a field onto a 5×5 subgrid using the generic interface (resolves by the kind of vint/v):
use precision, only: wp
use subgrid,   only: calc_subgrid_array

real(wp) :: v(nx,ny)            ! coarse aa-node field
real(wp) :: vint(nxi,nxi)       ! subgrid for one cell
integer, parameter :: nxi = 5

do j = 1, ny
do i = 1, nx
    ! (im1,ip1,jm1,jp1) computed with the caller's boundary convention
    call calc_subgrid_array(vint, v, nxi, i, j, im1, ip1, jm1, jp1)
    ! ... use vint (e.g. fractional area above a threshold) ...
end do
end do
